description: Per-domain scoring criteria with level indicators for technical interview evaluation.

junior.label: Junior / Entry-Level
general_markers: Names tools and technologies correctly
Knows terminology and can define concepts
Explains what something does but not why or when
Follows documented procedures but cannot adapt
Gives textbook answers without practical nuance
Cannot articulate tradeoffs between approaches

mid.label: Mid-Level
general_markers: Applies concepts to specific scenarios
Explains why a particular approach is preferred
Considers false positives and operational impact
Has practical experience with real incidents or detections
Can adapt procedures to non-standard situations
Identifies some tradeoffs but may miss edge cases

senior.label: Senior
general_markers: Architects solutions with explicit tradeoff analysis
Considers attacker perspective and adversary adaptation
Designs for scale, maintainability, and team operations
Connects technical decisions to business impact
Proactively identifies risks not asked about
Demonstrates cross-domain awareness
Has led complex investigations or programs

staff.label: Staff / Principal
general_markers: Challenges the premise of the question
Reframes problems to find higher-leverage solutions
Demonstrates systemic and organizational thinking
Considers industry-wide implications
Identifies when standard approaches are insufficient
Proposes novel architectures or methodologies
Influences direction beyond their immediate team

junior_indicators: Can list IR phases (preparation, identification, containment, eradication, recovery, lessons learned)
Knows to preserve evidence before remediation
Names common IR tools (CrowdStrike, Splunk, etc.)
Understands concept of chain of custody
Can follow a basic IR playbook step by step
mid_indicators: Adapts triage approach based on alert type and asset criticality
Correlates data across multiple log sources during investigation
Makes informed containment decisions balancing risk and business impact
Communicates effectively with stakeholders during an incident
Understands lateral movement patterns and can trace attacker paths
Performs root cause analysis beyond 'the user clicked a link'
senior_indicators: Designs containment strategies for APT scenarios (coordinated, simultaneous)
Architects evidence collection at scale (automation, cloud forensics)
Quantifies data exposure and communicates to legal/executive teams
Considers regulatory implications (GDPR, breach notification timelines)
Leads cross-functional incident response across multiple teams
Builds IR metrics program (MTTD, MTTR, recurrence rate)
Designs post-incident review process that drives systemic change
staff_indicators: Challenges standard IR model assumptions (perimeter-based, reactive)
Architects assume-breach security models
Designs IR program for organizational resilience, not just incident resolution
Quantifies IR program ROI for board-level communication
Influences industry standards or community practices

junior_indicators: Can write a basic SIEM query (SPL, KQL)
Understands what a detection rule does
Names relevant data sources (Sysmon, Windows Event Logs, EDR)
Knows MITRE ATT&CK exists and can navigate it
Can identify common attack patterns at a high level
mid_indicators: Writes detection rules with appropriate specificity and context
Tunes rules to reduce false positive rates with data-driven approach
Maps detections to MITRE ATT&CK techniques (sub-technique level)
Tests detections using attack simulation tools (Atomic Red Team, CALDERA)
Understands data pipeline reliability and log source health
Can convert between detection formats (Sigma, SPL, KQL)
senior_indicators: Designs detection architecture beyond individual rules (layered, behavioral)
Implements detection-as-code with CI/CD pipeline
Builds coverage gap analysis driven by threat intelligence
Architects risk-based alerting with entity scoring
Considers detection decay and implements continuous validation
Evaluates cost per detection and makes data ingestion tradeoffs
Leads purple team exercises to validate detection coverage
staff_indicators: Challenges signature/IOC-based detection paradigm
Designs novel detection architectures (stream processing, edge detection)
Evaluates and influences the detection tool market direction
Builds detection engineering programs at organizational scale
Considers fundamental limitations of detection approaches
Influences industry standards (Sigma, OCSF)

junior_indicators: Understands order of volatility
Can perform basic disk imaging with write blocker
Knows common forensic tools (FTK, EnCase, Autopsy)
Understands chain of custody basics
Can examine basic Windows artifacts (Event Logs, Prefetch)
mid_indicators: Performs memory analysis with Volatility (knows key plugins)
Builds forensic timelines using multiple artifact sources (Plaso/log2timeline)
Analyzes Windows registry artifacts for user activity and persistence
Correlates filesystem, registry, and event log evidence
Handles USB device forensics (USBSTOR, SetupAPI, MountPoints2)
Documents analysis for formal reporting
senior_indicators: Detects anti-forensic techniques (timestomping, log clearing, secure deletion)
Performs advanced memory forensics (injected code extraction, rootkit detection)
Handles cloud forensics (AWS, Azure evidence collection)
Prepares evidence and analysis for legal proceedings
Architects forensic lab and evidence handling procedures
Trains junior analysts in forensic methodology
Designs automated evidence collection pipelines
staff_indicators: Expert witness capable (Daubert standard, cross-examination preparation)
Designs forensic architecture for enterprise scale
Addresses novel forensic challenges (container forensics, cloud-native)
Contributes to forensic tool development or methodology
Challenges assumptions about forensic soundness in modern environments

junior_indicators: Can follow an alert triage playbook
Uses SIEM for basic query and investigation
Understands SOC tier model and escalation basics
Documents investigations in ticketing system
Can identify common alert types and initial response
mid_indicators: Triages alerts with context (asset, user, business impact)
Designs and improves SOAR playbooks for common alert types
Tracks and reports on SOC metrics (MTTD, MTTR, FP rate)
Handles shift handoff with structured process
Adapts investigation approach for novel or uncommon alerts
Mentors Tier 1 analysts
senior_indicators: Designs SOC operational frameworks (escalation, on-call, empowerment)
Architects automation strategy balancing speed and accuracy
Builds SOC metrics program with quality measurement (not just speed)
Evaluates and selects SOC tooling with TCO analysis
Designs remote-first or follow-the-sun SOC operations
Addresses analyst burnout and career progression systematically
Presents SOC value to executive leadership
staff_indicators: Challenges traditional SOC tier model
Designs authority and empowerment frameworks balancing speed and safety
Articulates SOC ROI and risk reduction quantification for board
Rethinks SOC career paths and industry retention patterns
Evaluates platform consolidation vs best-of-breed at strategic level

junior_indicators: Can look up IOCs in threat intel platforms
Understands basic IOC types (IP, domain, hash, URL)
Knows what MITRE ATT&CK is and can look up techniques
Can read a threat intelligence report
Understands the intelligence cycle at a textbook level
mid_indicators: Enriches IOCs from multiple sources with confidence assessment
Maps threat actor TTPs to MITRE ATT&CK at sub-technique level
Creates actionable intelligence products for different audiences
Manages threat intel feeds with quality scoring
Integrates CTI into detection engineering workflow
Conducts basic infrastructure pivoting from IOCs
senior_indicators: Designs CTI program aligned to organizational intelligence requirements
Performs attribution analysis with methodology and confidence levels
Builds threat actor profiles that drive detection and hunting
Designs indicator confidence scoring models
Navigates false flag analysis in nation-state context
Measures and demonstrates CTI program value
Leads intelligence sharing with ISACs and peers
staff_indicators: Challenges IOC-based intelligence paradigm (pyramid of pain)
Designs TTP-focused intelligence program with strategic products
Evaluates industry-wide intelligence sharing challenges and proposes solutions
Defines when corporate CTI should and shouldn't attempt attribution
Shapes organizational security strategy through intelligence
Influences community intelligence practices and standards
